meta.title: IDAS Prompt Management
meta.subtitle: Configuration and governance layer for Allianz's AI document classification system

data.headline: Making AI classification something you can vouch for.
data.body: Allianz processes thousands of incoming documents every day — claims, invoices, legal notices, correspondence. IDAS is the internal system that uses AI to classify each one and route it to the right team. This tool is the layer that controls how that AI thinks. It lets business configurators write, test, and publish the prompts that drive classification — without ever involving engineering.

creative_direction.notes: Heavy, deliberate reveal — this is the weight of the problem. Symmetric ease conveys constraint.

data.headline: The bottleneck wasn't speed. It was certainty.
data.body:
Before IDAS, document triage was manual. Skilled claims handlers spent hours every day doing mechanical routing — reading documents, categorizing them, forwarding to the right queue. Not analysis. Not decision-making. Just sorting.

When the AI was introduced, the problem didn't disappear — it shape-shifted. The AI was fast. But when it was wrong, no one knew until something downstream broke. And when it was right, handlers still double-checked everything, because there was no way to know which result to trust. The volume was there. The confidence wasn't.

creative_direction.notes: Before items slide from left (old way), after items slide from right (new way). The left-right opposition is literal.

before.label: Before this tool
items: Prompt changes required an engineering ticket — 3+ day turnaround
No test environment — every change went straight to production
Confidence scores invisible: 55% looked the same as 95%
Misclassifications surfaced hours later, downstream, untraceable
Domain expertise lived in Slack threads and spreadsheets, never in the system

after.label: With IDAS prompt management
items: Business configurators write and publish prompts independently, same day
Safe sandbox: test against real documents before anything goes live
Three-tier color signal (green/amber/red) makes confidence instantly readable
Errors visible before publishing — not after something breaks downstream
Domain knowledge encoded directly into the system by the people who have it

creative_direction.notes: Items stagger in one-by-one — each scope point lands with weight. Measured timing reflects enterprise rigor.

data.headline: Not a claims tool. The layer that tells the AI how to read claims.

items.title: Users = business configurators
items.description: Not ops staff. Not ML engineers. People with deep domain knowledge who had never had a direct way to encode that knowledge into the system — until now. The interface had to work for someone who understood insurance documents fluently but had never written a line of code.

items.title: Engineering dependency eliminated
items.description: Before this tool, changing prompt logic required raising an engineering ticket and waiting 3+ days for deployment. Business teams had workarounds — spreadsheets, shared docs, informal Slack threads — that tracked what the prompts were supposed to do but couldn't actually change them.

items.title: Full lifecycle in one place
items.description: Write, test against real documents, get approval, publish, monitor performance. The tool needed to cover every stage — not just authoring. Without a safe testing environment and a clear publish gate, configurators couldn't trust what they were changing or when it went live.

creative_direction.notes: Slides in from right — feels like a pivot moment. The insight reframes the whole project.

data.headline: Users don't fear AI being wrong. They fear being wrong without knowing it.
data.body:
The early assumption was that the design problem was accuracy — help users write better prompts so the AI gets more classifications right. That shifted quickly once I got into conversations with the configurator team.

What came up wasn't frustration with incorrect outputs. It was frustration with invisible ones. When a classification came back wrong and a document went to the wrong queue, there was no alert, no signal. The error surfaced hours or days later, downstream — and by then it was hard to trace back.

That reframed everything. The goal wasn't to make the AI more accurate. It was to make the AI's uncertainty legible — so configurators could act on it, not just be surprised by it.
data.image_hint: Diagram: two identical classification result rows side-by-side — one 55% confidence, one 95% — both displaying with no visual difference, to illustrate the invisibility problem

creative_direction.notes: Three findings from configurator interviews — the cards make each insight discrete and scannable.

items.headline: Confidence was invisible
items.body: A 55% result and a 95% result looked identical. Configurators had no way to know which classifications to trust and which to question.

items.headline: Errors surfaced too late
items.body: Misclassifications only appeared downstream — hours or days later — after a document had already gone to the wrong queue and caused work for someone else.

items.headline: Knowledge was locked out
items.body: Domain experts knew exactly what the prompts should say. But without a safe way to test, most avoided touching them entirely — fear of breaking production.

creative_direction.notes: Slower stagger — each decision deserves to breathe. This is the core design rationale section.

data.headline: Three decisions that redefined what the product needed to be.

items.title: Confidence as color, not as a number
items.description: The AI returns a confidence score on every classification. Early designs surfaced this as a percentage (87%, 62%, 44%). In usability sessions, no one mentioned the number unprompted. When we asked about it directly, most said they assumed it was a system-internal metric — not something they were meant to act on. We replaced it with a three-tier color system: green for high confidence (80%+), amber for uncertain (60–79%), red for low (below 60%). Configurators could now scan a test result and know immediately which rows needed attention. The shift from data point to signal changed how people used the test output entirely.

items.title: A publish lifecycle, not instant deploy
items.description: The first version had a single 'Save' button. Saving a prompt meant it went live. Two configurators told us they kept a personal log of every change they made — in case something broke and they needed to roll back manually. There was no undo, no staging state, no safety net. The fear of breaking production meant most people avoided touching the prompts at all. We introduced a three-stage lifecycle: Draft (editing, not yet testable), Tested (validated against documents, not yet live), and Approved & Published (live in production). We also added a persistent warning — '⚠ Unpublished changes' — to surface any gap between what's been tested and what's actually running. The effect was immediate: teams that had been avoiding prompt edits for weeks started iterating daily.

items.title: Plain language over technical precision
items.description: Early iterations used ML-accurate terminology throughout the interface: 'inference threshold', 'classification schema', 'token boundary'. Every term was technically correct. None of them meant anything to a business configurator. We rewrote the entire UI layer in plain language — 'Save draft', 'Approve & Publish', 'Test against documents'. Placeholder names like {{CLASSIFICATION_HIERARCHY}} kept their technical identifiers but were annotated with plain descriptions inline. The goal was that someone with zero ML background could use the tool fluently after a 20-minute onboarding.

data.headline: What the AI sees — and what you couldn't.
data.description: Every document in this queue has already been classified. The confidence scores exist. Toggle the signal to see what was invisible before.

creative_direction.notes: Blur-in — clarity emerging after uncertainty. Mirrors the tool's promise of legibility.

data.headline: From data entry to exception handling.
data.body:
Configurators who had avoided touching prompts for weeks started iterating daily. The feedback loop that didn't exist before — draft, test against real documents, see the output, decide — became the core of how the team worked.

The deeper shift was in how people related to the AI. Before the tool, outputs were treated as final — you accepted them or you didn't. After, they were treated as drafts — things you could examine, interrogate, and push back on. That's the change that's hardest to quantify, and the one I'm most proud of.

data.text: The instinct is always to hide uncertainty. But hiding it doesn't make it go away — it just moves the cost downstream. Design for the moment the AI is wrong, not just the moment it's right.
